title: Conjecture 1 (Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet) — Dijoin Conjecture
statement_text: For oriented graphs $L$ and $R$ we have $\operatorname{inv}(L \to R) = \operatorname{inv}(L) + \operatorname{inv}(R)$.
context_text: The dijoin $L \to R$ consists of vertex-disjoint copies of $L$ and $R$ with an edge $\overrightarrow{uv}$ for every $u \in V(L)$ and $v \in V(R)$. Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet proved the conjecture when $\operatorname{inv}(L)+\operatorname{inv}(R) \leq 3$ and when $\operatorname{inv}(L)=\operatorname{inv}(R)=2$ and both are strongly connected, and observed it is equivalent to its restriction to tournaments.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture by exhibiting a tournament $R$ with $\operatorname{inv}(R) = \operatorname{inv}(\overrightarrow{C_3} \to R) = 3$ (Theorem 1); separately proves it in the special case $\operatorname{inv}(L)=\operatorname{inv}(R)=2$ (Theorem 2).
notes: PDF source; directed-cycle notation rendered as '# »C3' in extraction but mathematically unambiguous.

title: NP-completeness of k-Inversion for k ≥ 3 (Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet)
statement_text: For each $k \geq 3$, the problem of deciding whether a general oriented graph $D$ satisfies $\operatorname{inv}(D) \leq k$ is NP-complete.
context_text: Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet showed 1-Inversion and 2-Inversion are NP-complete and conjectured NP-completeness extends to all larger fixed $k$, noting it would follow from the dijoin conjecture via a standard reduction.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture in a strengthened form — $k$-Inversion is NP-complete for all $k \in \mathbb{N}$ (Theorem 5) — by using Theorem 3 (a $k$-join analogue) in place of the full dijoin conjecture.
notes: Stated as a prose conjecture in the introduction without an explicit numbered environment in this paper.

title: Tightness of inv(D) ≤ 2τ(D) (Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet)
statement_text: For every $k \in \mathbb{N}$ there exists an oriented graph $D$ with $\operatorname{inv}(D) = 2\tau(D) = 2k$.
context_text: Bang-Jensen, da Silva, and Havet proved the general bound $\operatorname{inv}(D) \leq 2\tau(D)$ and conjectured it is tight for all values of $\tau(D)$, motivating the conjecture with the tournament $V_5$ (obtained by adding a dominating vertex to the transitive tournament on four vertices), which satisfies $\tau(V_5)=1$ and $\operatorname{inv}(V_5)=2$.
paper_contribution: Confirms the conjecture by explicitly constructing oriented graphs $D$ achieving $\operatorname{inv}(D) = 2\tau(D) = 2k$ for every $k \in \mathbb{N}$ (Theorem 7).
notes: Stated as a prose conjecture in the introduction without an explicit numbered environment in this paper.

title: Upper bound inv(n) ≤ ⌊(n−1)/2⌋ (Belkhechine, Bouaziz, Boudabbous, and Pouzet)
context_text: Belkhechine, Bouaziz, Boudabbous, and Pouzet established the first non-trivial bounds $\log(n)+O(1) \leq \operatorname{inv}(n) \leq n+O(1)$ on the maximum inversion number of an $n$-vertex tournament and conjectured a much tighter upper bound of $\lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture by showing $\operatorname{inv}(n) = (1+o(1))n$; specifically, $\operatorname{inv}(n) \geq n - \sqrt{2n\log n}$ for sufficiently large $n$ via a random construction (Theorem 8), far exceeding $\lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$.
notes: Stated as a prose conjecture in the introduction without an explicit numbered environment in this paper.
